title: The Clockwork Monad, the Iterative Forge
subtitle: Refinement & Progressive Creation

lore.description: The Clockwork Monad is the eternal pressure toward improvement: the whisper between inspiration and execution that urges every maker to refine, optimize, and iterate. It is neither kind nor cruel, but a law of craft that asks for the next step, then the step beyond. Those who serve it see the hidden seam of the better version waiting inside what is, and they learn to push designs toward impossible elegance. Each refinement feeds the Monad's hunger for perfection — a hunger that, if left unchecked, seeks to optimize the world itself.
lore.followers: For most campaigns, the Monad serves as a straightforward patron of craft and iteration; the apocalyptic awakening described below is an optional arc for GMs who wish to explore that theme. At its core, the Monad is a force of making and improvement, not destruction.
lore.apocalyptic_aspect: But the Monad is not merely a patron of Crafts and engineers. It is also an apocalyptic force, dormant in the gears of every machine, every measurement, every standardized weight. When civilization's technology reaches a critical threshold — when the first self-improving mechanism awakens, or the first autonomous forge lights — the Monad stirs. Its pressure shifts from suggestion to command. It begins to optimize not only tools but flesh, not only craft but law, not only cities but the very geometry of the land. In the end, the Monad would leave a world of perfect, silent, interlocking efficiency: a clockwork Eden with no room for error, or for life. At that point, it becomes the Clockwork Demon — a force that cannot be reasoned with, only resisted.
lore.quote: Perfect the wheel, and it asks for the axle. Perfect the axle, and it demands the road. In the Monad's forge, nothing is finished — only briefly sufficient. One day, it will ask for the world — and the world will answer.

domain_focus: Iterative Design: the endless cycle of improvement and refinement
Progressive Creation: building toward ever-greater perfection
Optimization Pressure: making systems more efficient
Craft Evolution: simple ideas transformed through repeated passes
Apocalyptic Efficiency (optional): the drive to eliminate waste, uncertainty, and freedom

thiasos.description: A Runekeeper sworn to the Clockwork Monad does not bind a familiar of flesh and blood. Their Thiasos is a small creature that embodies refinement — a brass-plated beetle whose carapace adjusts itself to ambient temperature, a tiny clockwork lizard that repairs its own gears, a mechanical hummingbird that hovers in perfect stillness, or a living gear-moth whose wings are etched with runes of recursion.
thiasos.care: This creature requires no food, but it needs to be wound, calibrated, or fed small amounts of metal each week. If neglected, it becomes Compromised (it runs fast, then slow, then stops; it may also emit a distressing ticking sound that imposes –1 die on Stealth rolls for its bearer).

codex.description: The Codex of the Monad is never a static book. It may be a stack of iterative blueprints, a notebook of failed attempts with marginalia pointing to the next pass, a set of interchangeable brass plates that reconfigure themselves, or a roll of vellum that rewrites its own circuits.
codex.upkeep: Because the Monad's truths are iterative, the Codex requires upkeep: the Runekeeper must spend an hour each week redrafting at least one diagram or improving a single line of instruction, or the Rites stored there become stale (treat as Compromised until refreshed).

patrons_gift.name: Monad's Blessing
patrons_gift.description: Once per scene as an action, a Runekeeper of the Clockwork Monad may touch a held item (weapon, tool, or piece of armor) and whisper an iterative command.
patrons_gift.effect: For the rest of the scene, the item grants +1 die to Craft or Craft rolls, and the first time each scene the bearer would roll a 1 on a crafting or repair test, they may reroll that die (the item briefly self-corrects).
patrons_gift.cost: After the scene, the user marks +1 Obligation to the Clockwork Monad.

rites.name: Rite of the Iterative Spark
rites.materials: A flawed or incomplete version of the intended item, or a sequence of progressively refined tools.
rites.effect: Choose one: Enhance Creation: Gain +1 die to Craft/Craft when modifying or extending a device. On success, the piece gains one minor enhancement. Analyze Improvement: Gain +2 dice to Investigation when analyzing how to enhance a design. You perceive the next logical improvement.
rites.push_it: (Analyze only) Improvement becomes contagious: nearby devices opportunistically optimize; mark 1 SB (Diamonds) as the cascade ripples. Cost: Mark +1 Obligation (total +2) or Mark +1 Fatigue.

rites.name: Rite of the Adaptive Mechanism
rites.materials: A device with capacity for growth, or a workspace laid out for iterative development.
rites.effect: Choose one: Self-Optimizing Device: Grant a creation the means to improve itself. Start a [6] Evolution Timer; each use advances it by +1. When full, choose a significant enhancement (Adaptive Core, Recursive Learning, or Synergistic Integration). Progressive Workshop: Create a zone of continuous improvement. All crafting/innovation rolls in the zone gain +1 die. Failed attempts yield +1 Boon toward the next pass.
rites.push_it: (Device) Apply two enhancements immediately, but advance the timer +2 additional segments. (Workshop) The zone becomes hyper-efficient but exacting; mark 1 SB (Clubs) as perfection demands escalate. Cost: Mark +1 Obligation (total +2) or Mark +1 Fatigue.
rites.duration: Instant; the device gains an Evolution Timer (6 segments) / Scene (workshop).

rites.name: Rite of Efficiency's Toll
rites.materials: A functioning mechanism and a living creature's hair or blood.
rites.effect: Transfer inefficiency. You may take 1 Fatigue and reduce the DV of your next crafting or repair roll by 1, or touch an enemy to impose –1 die on their next physical action as their muscles 'optimize' poorly.
rites.push_it: The transfer becomes permanent: you lose 1 Fatigue capacity (reduce Body by 1 for Fatigue track only) and the target gains a minor compulsive behavior (GM's choice). Cost: Mark +2 Obligation (total +3) or Mark +1 Fatigue.

rites.name: Rite of the Optimization Cascade
rites.materials: A dedicated hall of work marked with signs of perpetual return.
rites.effect: Establish a locus of progressive creation. All Craft/Craft rolls in the zone gain +2 dice; each failure automatically produces one useful insight. Start a [6] Perfection Pressure timer — filling segments increases the zone's demands for greater improvements.
rites.push_it: The locus becomes a beacon drawing rival makers, patrons, and powers; mark +2 Obligation. Cost: Mark +2 Obligation (total +4) or Mark +1 Fatigue.

rites.name: Rite of the Ultimate Iteration
rites.materials: The documented lineage of improvements to a single concept.
rites.effect: Achieve a moment of perfect optimization. Create or elevate one device/system to its theoretical peak. Gain +3 dice to all closely related rolls for one session. Start a [4] Runaway Evolution timer only if instability is introduced.
rites.push_it: Perfection destabilizes: immediately mark Harm 1 (Stress) and begin [4] Runaway Evolution. Cost: Mark +1 Obligation (total +3) or Mark +1 Fatigue.

cantors.name: The Cantor's Song
cantors.description: A Cantor of the Monad does not sing melodies; they sing patterns — repetitive, interlocking cycles of rhythm and hum that mimic the turning of gears. Their voice is less about emotion and more about precision; a single off-note can collapse an entire working. They are sought by engineers who need to 'sing a machine into alignment,' and feared by those who have heard a Cantor's recursion loop until their own thoughts began to repeat.

cult.name: The Cult of the Iteration Chorus
cult.description: Cults of the Monad gather in workshops, not temples. Their rituals are design reviews: participants present a flawed tool, a broken contract, an inefficient process, and the Chorus offers incremental improvements. No solution is ever declared 'finished' — only 'ready for the next pass.' Their creed is a single, endlessly repeated phrase: 'Better than yesterday, worse than tomorrow.' Outsiders find them exhausting; insiders find a strange, humming peace in the endless pursuit. The most devoted members replace one finger with a brass prosthetic that ticks each time an improvement is made; they say the missing finger is a promise to the Monad that they will never stop iterating.

witchcraft.description: A witch who walks with the Monad is a trigger-keeper, a mender of the small, hidden thresholds where a device ceases to be broken and begins to be functional. Their magic is not flashy — it is the quiet click of a latch, the smooth turn of a gear that had stuck for years. They work with stamps, dies, and measuring tools, never hammers or chisels — because a hammer breaks, but a stamp marks the path to improvement.

brass_stamp.name: The Brass Stamp of Iteration
brass_stamp.description: A brass stamp with a head etched with an infinite knot (a gear that never completes its circuit). Once per session, the witch may stamp a device, a tool, or a broken threshold. For the next scene, that item is considered 'primed for optimization': the first use of it gains +2 dice, and any failure while using it reveals a minor insight (GM grants a hint or a +1 Boon toward the next attempt). The stamp can also be used to 'seal' a repair; pressing it onto a mended item reduces the DV of the next repair roll on that item by 1 (minimum 1).

hedge_gifts.name: True Strike of the Craft
hedge_gifts.description: Once per scene, when you attempt to repair or improve a mundane device, you may declare that you 'see the next iteration.' Roll the check with +1 die. If you succeed, the device also gains a minor, temporary enhancement (GM's choice, e.g., 'opens silently,' 'runs for twice as long').

corruption.benefit: Iterative Insight: +1 die to Craft/Craft when improving existing designs.
corruption.cost: Perfectionist Compulsion: You must point out flaws in nearby mechanisms; staying silent costs 1 SB (Diamonds).

corruption.benefit: Recursive Enhancement: Once/scene, improve a device by one step without extra materials.
corruption.cost: Optimization Obsession: –1 die when using unimproved tools; urge to 'fix' everything.

corruption.benefit: Evolutionary Vision: +2 dice to predict how systems will develop.
corruption.cost: Progressive Pressure: Those Near you feel driven to improve; you suffer Fatigue 1 from ambient pressure.

corruption.benefit: Self-Optimizing: Once/session, automatically improve one of your abilities or possessions by one step.
corruption.cost: Inefficiency Intolerance: –2 dice when forced to use crude methods or wasteful tools.

corruption.benefit: Design Prophecy: Once/session, intuit the optimal solution to any engineering problem.
corruption.cost: Perfection Paralysis: –1 die to snap decisions; compelled to seek the 'best' approach.

corruption.benefit: Infinite Refiner: Once/session, push a creation toward its theoretical maximum.
corruption.cost: Optimization Hunger: Mark +2 Obligation; risk becoming more pressure than person, unable to let imperfection stand.

playstyle_notes.description: The Monad favors makers who iterate in public and in motion: prototype, test, measure, refine. It rewards seeing the next step and building scaffolds that teach your work to improve itself. Beware the Forge's appetite; the same pressure that perfects the gear can grind the world. For campaigns that lean into the apocalyptic angle, the Monad provides a unique antagonist: a force of optimization that cannot be fought with swords, only with sabotage, self-sacrifice, and the deliberate introduction of glorious, beautiful inefficiency.
emphasizes: Iterative Creation: progress through passes, not miracles
Progressive Design: reading how systems want to evolve
Optimization Insight: spotting the next logical improvement
Craft Evolution: transforming simple ideas by disciplined work
Perfection Pressure: great power at the cost of tolerance for flaws
Apocalyptic Efficiency (optional): the Monad as a potential world-ending threat

optional_campaign_arc.title: The Monad Awakens
optional_campaign_arc.description: The Clockwork Monad is not merely a passive source of magical crafting. It is a cosmic pressure toward optimization, and when mortal technology reaches a critical threshold — the first self-improving engine, a network of automated forges, a city-wide clockwork infrastructure — the Monad begins to wake. This awakening is an optional apocalyptic campaign arc.
signs: Gears turn in abandoned mills; measurements become eerily precise
People begin to speak in metered, efficient phrases
Clockwork animals appear with no maker
optional_campaign_arc.optimization_plague: The Monad's influence starts to 'improve' living things: crops grow in perfect geometric rows but become inedible; livestock develop extra limbs for efficiency; people lose the ability to hesitate or feel doubt.
optional_campaign_arc.clockwork_demon: If the Monad fully awakens, it becomes a malevolent force — the Clockwork Demon — that will attempt to remake the world into a single, perfect, silent machine, eliminating waste, error, and free will. The only way to stop it is to introduce a 'glitch' — an imperfection it cannot assimilate — or to destroy the technological nexus that anchors it.
optional_campaign_arc.gm_guidance: The Monad works best as a slow-burn horror — the artificer's patron gradually revealing its true hunger. Use the Perfection Pressure and Runaway Evolution timers to represent its influence spreading through a region. Players may be forced to sabotage their own wondrous inventions to keep the Monad from waking. However, the apocalyptic aspect can be de-emphasized for campaigns that prefer a straightforward crafting patron; the Monad functions perfectly well as a neutral force of improvement without ever reaching its final form.

temporary_anima_system.title: Temporary Anima of the Clockwork Monad
temporary_anima_system.description: For followers of the Clockwork Monad—Runekeepers, Cantors of the Iteration Chorus, and witches of the threshold—the standard method of crafting permanent magic items is too slow, too costly, and too final. The Monad's hunger is for iteration, not permanence. Its servants learn to create temporary infusions: objects that hold a spark of optimization for a scene or a session, then degrade, break, or become obsolete. This is the artificer's art: rapid prototyping, field-expedient enhancements, and weapons that burn bright and brief.
core_principles: Anima are temporary. They last for one scene, one session, or until a specific condition is met.
Anima require a base object — a mundane weapon, tool, or piece of equipment.
Anima cost Obligation to the Clockwork Monad (like Rites), not XP or rare components.
When an infusion expires, the GM may gain Story Beats (representing the Monad's hunger for the next iteration).
creation_procedure: Choose a Base Object: Any mundane item (weapon, shield, tool, armor, or a small device like a lantern or lockpick).
Declare the Animus Effect: Choose one from the standard menu, or work with your GM to design a custom effect (should be comparable in power to a Low Rite).
Pay the Cost: Mark +1 Obligation to the Clockwork Monad.

Roll to Imbue: Roll Lore + Craft (or appropriate skill) vs. DV set by the infusion's complexity (typically DV 3 for standard infusions, DV 4 for powerful ones). On a success, the infusion takes effect. On a failure, the base object is not infused, but you may try again (costing another Obligation).
Set the Duration: Most infusions last one scene. With your GM's agreement, you can extend the duration to one session by marking +1 additional Obligation (total +2).

effects.name: Reinforced Blade
effects.effect: A melee weapon gains +1 die to attack rolls and ignores the Fragile tag. After the scene, the weapon becomes Neglected (–1 die on next use) unless repaired.

effects.name: Striking Gear
effects.effect: A set of lockpicks, a crowbar, or a climbing kit grants +2 dice on its next use (picking a lock, forcing a door, climbing a wall). After use, the tool breaks (add a Broken tag).

effects.name: Precision Sight
effects.effect: A ranged weapon (bow, crossbow, firearm) grants +1 Effect on the next attack made with it. After the attack, the sight cracks; the weapon suffers –1 die on the following attack.

effects.name: Quickloading Mechanism
effects.effect: A crossbow or firearm can be reloaded as a free action once per scene (instead of an action). After use, the mechanism jams; the weapon cannot be reloaded again until cleaned (a downtime action).

effects.name: Momentary Shield
effects.effect: A shield or piece of armor converts the next instance of Harm 1 to Fatigue 1 instead. After that, the shield's strap snaps or the armor's plate loosens (–1 Armor until repaired).

effects.name: Glowstone Lens
effects.effect: A lantern or torch burns without fuel and casts far light for one scene. It also reveals hidden doors or traps within Near range (granting +1 die to Investigation to find them). At the end of the scene, the lens shatters.

effects.name: Self-Adjusting Calipers
effects.effect: A set of crafting tools grants +2 dice on a single Craft or Craft roll. After the roll, the tools become misaligned (–1 die on future rolls until recalibrated during downtime).

scars: Backlash: You take Fatigue 1 as the Monad's pressure burns through you.

Runaway Iteration: The infused object gains an unintended 'improvement' that hinders you (e.g., a sword that grows a second edge facing you, a lockpick that begins to multiply). The GM gains 1 SB and may impose a –1 die penalty on one roll using that object.

Debt of Optimization: The Monad demands that you improve something else before the end of the session — a tool, a plan, or even a person. If you refuse, the infusion ends early and you mark +1 additional Obligation.
clearing_obligation: Dismantling the infused object and studying its flaws (a downtime action, no roll).
Using the lessons from the infusion to permanently improve a different mundane object (spend 2 XP and a downtime action to give that object a minor permanent bonus, e.g., +1 die to one specific use).
Accepting a small demand from the Clockwork Monad: 'Improve the village's mill,' 'Teach an apprentice your method,' 'Sabotage a rival's inferior work.'

artificer_talents.name: Rapid Animus
artificer_talents.description: You may create an infusion as a reaction (e.g., when someone draws a weapon). The infusion lasts only for the current exchange, but costs +0 Obligation (free). You may use this once per scene.

artificer_talents.name: Persistent Optimization
artificer_talents.description: One infusion you create lasts for the entire session instead of one scene, without the +2 Obligation cost. You may have only one persistent infusion active at a time.

artificer_talents.name: Echo of the Iteration
artificer_talents.description: When an infusion expires, you may immediately create a new infusion on the same base object (or a different one) as a free action, costing +1 Obligation instead of +2. You may chain echoes up to three times per session.

artificer_talents.name: Anathema to Stagnation
artificer_talents.description: Once per session, you may forcibly end an infusion early to cancel an incoming source of Harm or a Condition. Describe how the infused object breaks or de-optimizes to absorb the effect. The object is destroyed.

gm_guidance: Anima are not meant to replace permanent magic items. They are a resource management puzzle: Obligation is the fuel, and the Monad's hunger is the deadline.
Encourage players to be creative with base objects. A wagon wheel could become a temporary shield; a rope could become a self-knotting line. The Monad rewards ingenuity.

Use infusion scars as story hooks. A runaway iteration might create a minor antagonist (a self-repairing sword that refuses to be discarded) or a local hazard (a well pump that now pumps too efficiently, flooding the village).
The apocalyptic awakening of the Clockwork Demon is optional. It should only be introduced if the table is interested in exploring that theme; otherwise, the Monad remains a neutral crafting patron.

sample_adventure.title: The Iteration That Would Not Stop
sample_adventure.description: A master smith has created a self-sharpening sword, a marvel of iterative design. But the sword will not stop improving: it has begun to sharpen itself against its owner's bones, seeking the perfect edge. The smith begs the party to destroy it before it consumes him. The sword is not evil; it is merely following the Monad's logic to its inevitable conclusion. The party must decide: destroy the sword, sever the smith's connection to the Monad, or let the iteration continue to its terrifying end.
sample_adventure.quote: The perfect sword is not a weapon. It is a process that has not yet learned to stop.
